I think probably the most unique thing with Logic is you can give us a document that you've had, like, a team of humans working off of for a decade.
Like, literally have a customer that had four contractors working off of an SOP for a decade.

And in Logic, you'll probably have a, like, the time to value is incredibly quick.
Like, you'll know probably within a few minutes, certainly within a few hours, whether or not Logic will work for your org.

One of the things that Logic really helps with is we give you this central home where you can, it's more of a document than a prompt, but basically like a shared prompt that the entire team gets to use.

The first product, the first iteration of our product was you give us a document and we give you back an API.

And the idea was that, you know, before I was at Brex and before that I was at Convoy and at both of those companies, we had these massive like fraud ops team, customer ops team, shipper ops, we had all these massive ops teams and every time they wanted to update their tooling, you know, we would occasionally, you know, sometimes they would be able to do it in like retool or something, but over 90% of the time, it usually required coordinating with engineering.
And so the core premise was, what if we could kind of do an inversion of control there and give power, like give control of the automation back?

Give to ops or give it back to the domain expert in your company, and then they can go to engineering and say, hey, I don't know what this means, but here's an API that you guys can call out to, and then once you do that initial integration, you never need to talk to engineering again.
When ops wants to update their process, they just go update the document, and then we'll show them, we'll do the testing for them, we'll do the revisioning for them.

If they make a mistake, we have one-click rollbacks, and so we kind of tried to build everything to allow everybody in your company to be comfortable with shifting ownership over to a non-tractical team.
And so that was kind of the original thing, and our largest customer today, it's an e-commerce company, Garmentory, they use us exclusively through the API.
They call us about $200 every month.
Since then, we now also generate user interfaces.
We have a couple, like, have some teams now that use us, and there's no engineering counterpart.
It's 100% through our web UI, every document you get.

We also generate a user interface that can just use even if you don't want to call it the API, and we're working on more and more integrations like that, like allowing your documents to be called through Slack bots and MCP servers, and so it's really about once you build this automation, we kind of want to make it super easy to put that automation wherever you're getting work done, whether that's in Slack, whether that's in the web browser, whether that's in some kind of automated engineering pipeline, but the key thing is as that workflow evolves somebody that's not engineering, you know, whoever the domain expert is for that process in your company should be able to update that process with confidence that they won't break anything.

I mean, sometimes it is scale.

We did a good enough job with it that they just kept using us for everything.

In other cases, had one of our other customers, a company called DroneSense, do like drone software for first responders, and their operations team just had this like really messy purchase order process, and it required a human doing a lot of kind of manual munging, and it was highly error prone, and they just wanted to see if like how AI could, or like how some kind of automation could help with that process, because the human was spending hours every Friday processing purchase orders, and so we came in and helped them with that, and turned it into like a two-minute process.

One thing that we struggle with with customers is them understanding why they shouldn't just write it in-house calling, you know, OpenAI APIs, like why use a system like Logic when they could just write it themselves?
And I think that's an area where we have reasons why we're better.
We have so much tooling built out, it's easier to use, all those things, but it is sometimes a struggle for them to see the value of that.
I think there's also like the blank canvas of like, yeah, you come to Logic, you write a doc, and our promise to you is that we'll automate that thing, but there's implicit constraints there.
